---
title: "Spiking Neural Networks"
description: "Test"
listing:
  contents: "."                # Look inside this current folder for posts
  sort: "date desc"            # Sort chronologically, newest first
  type: default                # Grid or list style options: default, grid, table
  categories: true             # Allow sidebar tag filtering within this project
---

Test

<!-- The first thing that got me into machine learning was [MarI/O](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qv6UVOQ0F44). I was absolutely blown away at how such a basic set of elements (neurons) could be connected in a way that made Mario blaze through the level unscathed. Even more mind blowing was that this set of connections could be obtained through an evolutionary process. No direct design. Just trial and error and environmental pressures. Ever since then, I have been obsessed with evolutionary optimization methods and have tried to sneak them into my work whenever I can. 

These days, (neuro)evolutionary optimization methods have somewhat fallen to the wayside in favor of the almighty back propagation algorithm, and for good reason. Evolutionary methods can be very sample inefficient, resulting in significantly worse performance as compared to back propagation for the same number of training passes. Back propagation based methods are able to do something truly amazing. They constantly move towards a better solution in way that also seemingly avoids local optima. The only catch is that they require that the network be differentiable.

As the current explosion of AI took off, I drifted away from neuroevolution and spent my time learning about other kinds of interesting emergent phenomenon. I never stopped being interested in it. But the massive success of the current back propagation paradigm made it seem like I was barking up the wrong tree. 

In come spiking neural networks. I don't know how, but up until a couple weeks ago I did not know that these existed. Instead of outputting a single continuous value, the neurons in a spiking neural network output discrete spatio-temporal spikes. This makes direct application of back propagation ineffective. The time for neuroevolution has come. 

The goal of this project is for me to learn how to use artificial spiking neural networks (SNNs) and experiment with different ways of training them. I have a million questions, but the major ones I want to answer with this project are the following
* What libraries are out there and which one should I use for neruoevolution type projects?
* How are SNNs trained?
* How do SNNs store memories?  -->
